coredata.dc:title: Heteroatom doped graphene engineering for energy storage and conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Rajesh
Sahoo, Sumanta
Joanni, Ednan
Singh, Rajesh K.
Maegawa, Keiichiro
Tan, Wai Kian
Kawamura, Go
Kar, Kamal K.
Matsuda, Atsunori
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom-doped graphene and its derived layered materials play a substantial role in several emerging science fields, demonstrating great potential for implementation in new devices and for improving the performance of existing technologies. A wide variety of strategies have been applied for the controlled synthesis and for achieving the intended doping/co-doping levels in the carbon network of graphene-based materials. Precise and reproducible doping is crucial for altering the Fermi energy level and to tune the band gap according to the desired device/application. Heteroatom-doped and co-doped graphene-based materials (n-type and p-type doping) have been synthesized for devices in energy-related applications using various chemical and physical routes. In this review article, we survey the most recent research works on the synthesis of heteroatom-doped graphene materials such as reduced graphene oxide, graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots and graphene nanoribbons. Applications of these materials in energy storage/conversion devices (supercapacitors, batteries, fuel cells, water splitting and solar cells) are also reviewed. Finally, the challenges and future perspectives for heteroatom-doped graphene materials are briefly discussed. We hope this article offers a useful starting point for researchers entering the field, providing an overview of synthesis approaches and energy applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped graphene-based materials and their energy-related applications.

Besides being an important component of hybrid electrode materials, GO is widely used as an intermediate precursor for obtaining doped rGO. The image shows the location of oxygen-containing different functional groups in the graphitic network, as well as the favorable characteristics of rGO for electrodes in electrochemical devices (after different degrees of reduction).

These schematic drawings show possible positions for dopant atoms in the graphene network, as well as the configurations of the most important functional groups. In each case, the normal and cross-sectional views of the doped material are provided for easier visualization and comparison. For the sake of clarity, this simplified representation does not include the actual number of chemical bonds between the elements.

Schematic illustration of some of the most promising methods applied to the synthesis of doped graphene materials for energy storage and conversion devices. For each method, the carbon and dopant precursors are shown, as well as the type of vessel needed for synthesis. Additional operations/components and the energy inputs are also indicated.

In broad terms, N-atom has been considered by many researchers as the most effective dopant for electrochemical energy-related applications. This is probably true, as far as energy storage devices are concerned. The image above highlights some of the most important consequences of N-doping for SCs and LIBs based on doped graphene. The positions and nomenclature for the most common N groups in graphene materials are also shown as a visual aid.
Main effects of doping graphene, with each column representing different heteroatoms. The top rows show positive effects, while the bottom rows show negative aspects.

Schematic drawing of a hypothetical integrated electrochemical energy conversion and storage system, with indication of the main processes/reactions taking place at the electrodes. All the devices shown in the image can benefit from the improved performance afforded by doped graphene electrodes. Adapted with modifications from Ref. [438].
Electronic structure-related data for different dopants for carbon.
Heteroatom doping in graphene-based materials and its positive contributions for energy-related applications.

row.entry: Improved electrochemical performance; decreased charge-transfer resistance; enhanced interlayer separation; better conductivity; band gap opening, suitable for specific device application.

Enhanced electrochemical stability; improved interface between graphene and metal oxide nanoparticles; enables high and uniform loading of nanoparticles; achieving balance between Li/Na binding and diffusion

Superior stability of Pt electrocatalysts in doped structure; induced charge polarization and spin density improves oxygen adsorption and cleavage; enables abundant and uniform loading of metal nanoparticles
Improved electrocatalytic activity; provides good supporting matrix for semiconductor-based photocatalysts to enhance photocatalytic activity
Enhanced adsorptive and charge-transfer abilities toward reduction in DSSC; increases catalytic activity of DSSC counter electrodes; increases graphene work function

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Main characteristics of the methods for heteroatom doping graphene-based materials, including reported doping limits.

row.entry: Simultaneous growth and doping; large area monolayer, bi-layer graphene formation; good control of doping levels; uniform layers
High operating temperature; danger of hazardous gas formation; high cost and low yield
Large scale production; no catalysts required
High voltage/current; low doping levels; few/multi-layer defective graphene formation
Short reaction time and low power consumption
Wide variety of dopant precursors; good control of doping levels
Doping occurs at edges; difficult to control the doping process; high amount of oxygen as impurity
Low cost, low temperature; large scale production; easily achieved doping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Common dopant sources for graphene-based materials.
Synthetic methods and processing conditions for heteroatom-doped graphene-based materials applied to energy storage and conversion devices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped graphene and its derived layered materials play a substantial role in several emerging science fields, demonstrating great potential for implementation in new devices and for improving the performance of existing technologies. A wide variety of strategies have been applied for the controlled synthesis and for achieving the intended doping/co-doping levels in the carbon network of graphene-based materials. Precise and reproducible doping is crucial for altering the Fermi energy level and to tune the band gap according to the desired device/application. Heteroatom-doped and co-doped graphene-based materials (n-type and p-type doping) have been synthesized for devices in energy-related applications using various chemical and physical routes. In this review article, we survey the most recent research works on the synthesis of heteroatom-doped graphene materials such as reduced graphene oxide, graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots and graphene nanoribbons. Applications of these materials in energy storage/conversion devices (supercapacitors, batteries, fuel cells, water splitting and solar cells) are also reviewed. Finally, the challenges and future perspectives for heteroatom-doped graphene materials are briefly discussed. We hope this article offers a useful starting point for researchers entering the field, providing an overview of synthesis approaches and energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a material with an essentially bi-dimensional (2D) layered structure, has attracted significant research as a high-performance electrode component in devices for energy applications [1–7]. The outstanding properties of graphene, such as its electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, surface area, thermal conductivity and optical transmittance in the visible-infrared region makes it the most exciting material to emerge in this century [8–20]. In order to further improve its performance in the field of energy-related devices, considerable attention has been paid to the surface/structural modification of graphene [21–32]. The numerous approaches for modification include combination with polymers,metal nanoparticles, metal oxides and doping the graphitic carbon network with heteroatoms [33–37].

By doping graphene-based materials, one can efficiently tune their electronic structure and intrinsic properties, significantly affecting chemical reactions taking place at the interfaces, resulting in high electrochemical performance [38–44]. Heteroatom doping in graphene, graphene nanosheets (GNSs), graphene nano-ribbons (GNRs), graphene hydrogel, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene oxide (GO), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has been vigorously researched, especially for energy-related applications [45–52]. Several physical and chemical approaches have been studied for heteroatom doping in graphene-based materials [53–58]. Among the heteroatoms, N doping has been shown to effectively improve the capacitance of electrodes [59–63]. It has also been established that co-doping with two or more types of heteroatoms yields even higher capacitances due to synergistic effects [64–67].

From the viewpoint of materials science, this article first presents an overview of the recent progress on strategies for heteroatom doping graphene-based materials. Applications of doped graphene in supercapacitors (SCs), batteries (Li-ion batteries: LIBs; Li-S batteries: LSBs; Na-ion batteries: SIBs), fuel cells, solar cells (dye-sensitized solar cells: DSSCs; flexible organic solar cells: FOSCs) and water splitting are then presented. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation for different heteroatoms doping in graphene derivatives for energy applications. The text is concluded with a brief discussion of the challenges and future perspectives for doped graphene-based materials in energy-related applications.

The doping modulation of graphene can proceed following two different (but non-excluding) paths: (1) replacement of C atoms from the graphene network with heteroatoms and (2) attachment of dopants to the graphene surface through adsorption processes. Among these two, substitutional doping is relatively more stable due to the presence of covalent bonding between atoms in the carbon lattice and heteroatoms. On the other hand, one must keep in mind that, depending on the dopant atom and its concentration, the electrical/electrochemical properties may actually deteriorate. A fine balance must be found between defects providing charge carriers (or increasing their mobility) and defects disrupting the conduction pathways. Ultimately, there is even the possibility of destroying the honeycomb structure of graphene due to an excessive number of defects introduced by doping.

Among the different elements, B, N, P and S have been considered as the more appropriate dopants for graphene due to their similarities with the carbon atoms. Table 1 allows a comparison of their sizes, electronic configurations, number of valence electrons, and electronegativities. The parameters for carbon atoms have also been included for comparison.

As seen in Table 1, the size of B (in terms of Van der Waals radius) and its number of valence electrons (one less) are similar to those of C. Only minute changes have been observed in the lattice parameters of B-doped graphene, due to the slightly larger bond length for B-C when compared to C–C in the graphene lattice. On the other hand, N displays electron-rich characteristics when compared to B and C. Moreover, unlike B, N may experience both sp3 and sp2 hybridization. As a result, the planar structure of the graphene lattice may be distorted at high N doping levels. Additionally, the higher electronegativity of N (3.04) generates a polarization in the carbon network of graphene. When compared to N, P-doping generates more structural damage in graphene because of its larger size, but the electronegativity of P is lower than C. Moreover, the doping effect of P is found to be different from that of N, due to the additional number of orbitals in P. Unlike the other doping elements, S has electronegativity very similar to C, which results in minor polarization of the C-S bonds. The creation of defective sites in graphene upon S doping is generally caused by the bigger bond length of the C–S bond as compared to the C–C bond. This brief discussion illustrated some of the preliminary selection criteria for graphene dopants [41]. For each particular application, the working mechanisms of the devices should, of course, be also taken into account. Recently, the theoretical aspects of the doping modulation in graphene have been well reviewed by Lee et al. [68].

Direct substitutional doping of an oxygen atom in the carbon network of graphene is very difficult due to its strong electronegativity and large size. The most versatile graphene derivative, GO, contains hydroxyl and epoxy functional groups at the basal plane and carbonyl and carboxyl groups at the edges. The covalent attachment of oxygen-containing functional groups turns sp2 into sp3 hybridization, with local distortion of the graphene planar structure. The presence of localized sp3 domains generates defects and excessive band gap opening, making GO a poor electrical conductor [69,70]. On the other hand, the oxygen-containing groups are easily modified by physical and chemical processing, making GO a highly convenient precursor for doped graphene [71–73]. Simultaneous GO doping and reduction has been exploited by several researchers, because the removal of oxygen-containing groups generates convenient reactive sites for easy introduction of heteroatoms. The modified electronic structure of doped rGO has been used to enhance the electrochemical performance of devices in many applications, including energy storage and conversion [74–77]. The advantages of GO as a convenient material for doped graphene synthesis, as well as the main oxygen-containing functional groups in GO are illustrated in Fig. 2 .

In order to provide a clear view of the changes in the graphene lattice upon doping, schematic drawings of the graphene network doped with heteroatoms are shown in Fig. 3 . Recently, B-doped graphene (BG), N-doped graphene (NG), P-doped graphene (PG), S-doped graphene (SG), B-N/N-S/B-P/N-P co-doped graphene and multi-atom-doped graphene have demonstrated a high performance in several fields, especially in energy applications [78–81]. Table 2 shows a summary of the advantages of doping graphene-based materials for energy-related applications.

For heteroatom-doping graphene-based materials, some common synthesis approaches are listed in Table 3 , which also includes precursors, type of dopant and its concentration, as well as some of the positive effects and limitations of each method. Fig. 4 shows the energy and precursor inputs needed for some of the more promising synthesis methodologies.

Different kinds of solids, liquids and gaseous substances (containing B, N, P or S atoms) have been suggested for heteroatom doping in graphene-based materials. Table 4 shows several examples of chemicals/precursors which have been used for this purpose. The table is arranged according to the type and number of doping elements.

When compared to carbon, B atoms are electron deficient, so B doping is expected to enhance the p-type conducting behavior of graphene-based materials [155–168]. Among the several methods already published for incorporating B in graphene-based materials, thermal annealing has been frequently adopted. Graphite oxide or GO are the preferred graphene precursors, while boric acid is the most convenient B source. In the process of GO reduction and B doping, defects are introduced and some of the oxygen-containing groups may still remain on the surface of B-rGO. The B atoms occupy the vacancies created by the removal of oxygen-containing groups or defects in the material structure. Hydrothermal processing, a wet chemical method combining moderately low temperatures and high pressures, is another widely used technique for synthesizing BG [169–173].

N-doping has been widely applied for obtaining n-type semiconducting graphene materials. NG has attracted great attention due to its high electrical conductivity and good chemical stability, with enhanced performance in energy-related devices [174–196]. Most of the N-doping can be classified as graphitic, pyridinic and pyrrolic [197–204]. Graphitic-N substitutes the C atoms in the graphene layer, bonding to three carbons in a sp2 configuration. Pyridinic-N is attached to two C atoms as part of hexagonal arrangements, while pyrrolic-N also bonds with two C atoms, but in this case taking part of pentagonal patterns. Both types of N are found at the edges of graphitic layers, but it is also possible for them to be associated with vacancies [205–214]. Synthesis of NG derivatives has been frequently accomplished by hydrothermal and solvothermal methods [215–229]. Doped GQDs have been mainly synthesized by hydrothermal and solvothermal methods using different carbon-containing precursors [230–236]. Fig. 5 shows some of the effects of N-doping and their influence on the main performance parameters for energy storage devices (batteries and SCs).

P-doping in graphene-based materials is energetically more favorable than N-doping because the formation energy of PG from gas dopant atoms is lower (7.1 eV/atom) [237] than that of NG (8.0 eV/atom) [136]. This indicates that P substitutional doping in graphene is attractive for obtaining n-type doped materials [238–244]. PG materials have been hydrothermally synthesized for several applications. Hydrothermal treatment followed by thermal annealing is another viable approach for the synthesis of PG [245–249].

Theoretical analysis has shown that S doping in graphene materials forms a thiophene-like structure, providing a positive influence on the electronic and magnetic properties of graphene [250]. SG displays good catalytic activity because S can easily transfer two electrons to C atoms in the graphene network, thus changing the band gap [159]. Several approaches have been proposed for the synthesis of n-type semiconducting SG using different types of gaseous, liquid and solid precursors. For synthesis of S-doped GQDs, different solution synthesis approaches have been suggested [251–255].

Co-doped graphene materials, with more electrochemically active sites, have distinctive electronic structures. Synergistic effects from co-doping usually lead to better donor performance and electrochemical properties. Accordingly, co-doped/multi-heteroatom doped graphene has been widely applied in energy storage and conversion devices [188,256–269]. The B and N atoms are nearly similar in size, but provide opposite doping effects in graphene-based materials (p-type & n-type, respectively). In the case of simultaneous B and N doping of graphene, boron nitride (BN) domains tend to form due to a phase separation between C and BN. There are larger binding energies in B–N and C–C bonds when compared to B–C and N–C bonds. [132,270–272]. B–N co-doped graphene-based materials show good reactivity. The strong charge polarization between B and N atoms is considered to be responsible for the active surface chemistry [41,270,273]. Graphene materials co-doped with N and S show excellent electrical conductivity and electrochemical performance when compared to materials doped by single elements [104,110,227,274–282]. Wet chemical processing, with doping taking place at room temperature or under heating is the preferred method. Variations of this approach may include high pressures and/or microwave heating. Besides graphene and rGO, GQDs have also been co-doped with N and S to improve the performance of electrodes in energy applications [283–289]. For synthesis of B–P, B–S, N–P and P–S co-doped graphene-based materials, several procedures have been developed, but most of the methods rely on chemical or thermal treatments (or a combination of both) [290–302]. Several studies have shown that multi-heteroatom doping was an effective approach for further improving the performance of graphene materials for energy applications [303–308]. Multi-heteroatom doped graphene (B-N-P) has been synthesized by annealing GO with different types of chemicals, such as NH4NO3, H8B10N2O16·8H2O, NH4H2PO4, B2O3 and P2O5 [306].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy related applications of heteroatom-doped graphene-based materials
ce:section.ce:para:
Apart from band-gap opening, heteroatom doping introduces significant changes in graphene materials. Examples of these changes are the structural deformation of graphene and the charge polarization between the dopant and carbon atoms. Further consequences (and benefits) of heteroatom-doping graphene-based materials for energy-related applications are listed below [309–314]:
1.
The incorporation of heteroatoms generates a large number of active sites, increasing reactivity, especially in the basal plane of graphene.
2.
The inclusion of heteroatoms can enhance the electronic conductivity of graphene.
3.
The creation of defects upon heteroatom-doping makes the graphene structure highly porous, enhancing the accessibility of electrolyte ions.
4.
In the co-doping process, synergistic effects among dopants can improve the electrochemical properties by generating additional non-neutral sites. Specifically for the SCs, the heteroatoms generate pseudocapacitance, which contributes to the global capacitance of devices.
5.
Heteroatom-doping increases the surface area by causing edge entanglement of graphene. For example, the large surface area and the additional active sites of NG are responsible for enhancing the ORR catalytic activity [315].
6.
Doping improves the wettability of graphene materials, which is not only essential for device applications, but also very favorable for most chemical processing methods.

Fig. 6 shows some of the main benefits/problems arising from doping in graphene materials, taking into account their application in energy devices. Relevant characteristics of each dopant atom in electrode materials are described.

Table 5 summarizes the synthesis methods for heteroatom-doped graphene and related materials. The data are presented as they were in the original articles and, whenever possible, include precursors, synthesis conditions and the intended energy-related application.

The pseudocapacitive contribution of dopants, the superior EDLC characteristics of graphene, and the enhanced electrical conductivity are responsible for improving the properties of doped graphene materials for SC devices [349–364]. Both doped and co-doped graphene materials have been applied as SC electrodes.

Among various heteroatoms, N is considered to be the most significant dopant for enhancing the capacitive properties of carbon materials, since the introduction of N generates extra sites for faradic redox reactions. Besides the pseudocapacitive contribution, N-doping alters the electronic structure of graphene, enhancing charge carrier density. Several studies have been published on the fabrication of NG for SC electrodes. As a general trend, both the pyrrolic and the pyridinic forms of N enhance the specific capacitance due to their pseudocapacitive contribution. On the other hand, graphitic N tends to increase the electrical conductivity of graphene, and the incorporation of large amounts of N atoms on the basal plane also increases the specific capacitance.

Synthesis of NG by hydrothermal reaction using (NH4)2HPO4 as the doping agent are described by Yu et al. [365]. In this work, the authors clearly demonstrated the role of N present at the edges of graphene layers and its effect on the capacitive properties. Additionally, they also confirmed the contribution of quinine oxygen to the pseudocapacitive nature of the doped electrode. The materials exhibited high capacitance as well as enhanced cycling stability in aqueous electrolytes. Liu et al. [366] used CH4N2O as the doping agent to synthesize porous NG. The authors observed a three-fold increase in the BET surface area of graphene upon N-doping. Using a non-aqueous electrolyte, the authors extended the energy and power density of doped graphene-based symmetric SCs. Flexible solid-state SC electrodes based on NG monoliths have also been reported [367]. In this work CH8ClN5 worked as N dopant and reducing agent for GO. The material exhibited high cycling stability and was mechanically robust. The NG aerogels have also been applied to SC electrodes [280,293,368–376]. Lei et al. [377] obtained enhanced electrochemical performance of NG cathodes for aluminum-ion hybrid SCs.

Combining the excellent properties of NG with other dopants, researchers have developed co-doped graphene-based materials for SC electrodes. For example, Cheng et al. [378] reported the radiolytic synthesis of N–S co-doped graphene, which exhibited long-term cycling stability up to 25,000 charge/discharge cycles. In this work, using C3H7NO2S as N–S co-dopant, the authors synthesized co-doped graphene from GO using electron-beam irradiation. The researchers attributed the observed enhancement of specific capacitance to the stronger irradiation dose, which increased the surface area and the pyrrolic-N content. The S content in the co-doped electrodes was lower than the N content because of the oversized atomic radius of S, which hindered its incorporation in the carbon network. In another report, Chen et al. [379] demonstrated good SC performance with long-term cycling stability for B–N co-doped graphene-like carbon. The electrodes were prepared by a ball-milling process using H3BO3 and N2 gas.

In order to improve the electrochemical properties of electrodes for high energy/power density SCs, nanohybrids based on doped graphene have also been synthesized. Wu et al. [380] have prepared nanocomposites based on NG and Fe2O3 using a hydrothermal process with a series of amino acids, i.e. aspartic acid (acidic), glycine (neutral), and lysine (basic) as the N-sources. Among these, NG from aspartic acid treatment showed a 3D porous structure. The agglomeration of graphene has been avoided through electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged GO and negative aspartic acid. A symmetric SC based on these electrodes exhibited better cycling stability and energy density through efficient proton transfer in the 3D structure of the hybrid.

The electrochemical performance of hybrid electrodes based on MnO2/Mn3O4 with NG has been studied by Cui et al. [381]. The electrode materials were prepared by hydrothermal processing, where C2H8N2 was used as the N source for doping. Using NG as an anchoring substrate, the MnO2-nanosheet covered Mn3O4 octahedrons achieved high capacitance. Along with the morphological factor, the enhanced electrochemical properties can be attributed to the reduced bulk resistance of the composite electrode. A similar work, focusing on free-standing binderless SC electrodes based on NG and MnO2 has also been reported [382]. Mohamed et al. [383] fabricated SC electrodes based on α-MnS and N–S co-doped rGO. Using C2H8N2 and CS2 as the N and S sources, respectively, the authors achieved cycling stability of more than 100% after 10,000 cycles. Li et al. [384] synthesized hollow NiCo2O4 nanorods on the surface of NG, which exhibited good electrochemical performance in a high concentration aqueous electrolyte. In another work, Wang et al. [385] fabricated high-performance SC electrodes by decorating the surface of NG with nickel oxide/nickel ferrite nanoparticles. The source of N for graphene doping in their research was CH4N2O.

Among the secondary batteries, LIBs are the most popular, but their relatively low energy density has prompted research on a variety of other types of batteries (LSBs, SIBs, etc.). Since the doped graphene-based electrodes were shown to display better electrochemical characteristics, they were naturally expected to improve battery performance [386–397].

The improved performance of N-doped LIB electrodes by synthesizing NG/SnO2 composites are demonstrated by Liang et al. [398]. The doped composites exhibited higher conductivity and better charge transfer kinetics. A ternary nanocomposite based on NG, SnS, and SnO2 has been demonstrated, exhibiting high cycling stability for LIB applications [399]. The uniform distribution of metal oxide and metal sulfide particles over the conductive network of NG drastically enhanced the pseudocapacitive contribution. The synergistic effects among the individual components resulted in highly reversible capacity and notable electrochemical properties. In a recent study, Zhang et al. [346] synthesized B–N co-doped graphene by a simple heat treatment process. By creating a huge number of defects, the doping process generated electrodes exhibiting large surface area and a higher number of active sites for Li storage, resulting in better LIB performance. A similar strategy has been adopted for application in SIBs. A co-doped N–P graphene aerogel has been synthesized by a hydrothermal-assisted freeze-drying method [400]. This aerogel showed enhanced rate capability and fast Na ion storage when tested as anode material. The authors explained the improved Na ion battery performance by the high surface area, enhanced conductivity and bigger capacitive contribution from the carbonaceous material.

Aerogels containing 3D NG/S composites for LSBs have been studied by Kang et al. [401]. The main object of this work was to fabricate the battery cathode by forming a stable, conductive interconnected network structure, with sulfur working as a stabilizer. The authors used C2H8N2 as the N-source, and doping was performed by a hydrothermal-assisted melt diffusion process. During the process of battery testing, NG restricted the suspension of polysulfides into the electrolyte and decreased the transport effects during cycling, so the electrodes displayed high reversible capacity. The authors also demonstrated that the NG/S composites displayed better rate capability and cycling stability than acetylene black/S composites. Su et al. [402] reported the synthesis of NG/CNT hybrid composites from Prussian blue for LSB applications. The hybrid electrodes exhibited a large number of defects, leading to long-term cycling stability up to 1000 cycles. Moreover, the 2D scaffold minimized shuttle effects and prevented the dissolution of polysulfides. Shi et al. [403] suggested that BG is highly efficient for improving LSBs. Graphene doping was achieved by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite in Li bis(oxalato)borate/dimethyl methylphosphonate electrolyte. With high conductivity, large number of active sites and better mechanical strength, this doped graphene exhibited enhanced capacity and long-term cycling stability in a LSB cathode.

NG-based materials have also been used as anodes for SIBs. Zhang et al. [404] synthesized binder-free NG-based anodes by laser scribing using CH4N2O and polyimide films. At comparatively high doping levels (12.8 at%), the enhanced SIB performance of this doped electrode was explained by its high surface area, 3D porous structure and enhanced conductivity. Quan et al. [405] reported the use of SG as anode material for SIBs. The authors used solvothermal-derived graphene as precursor with a more disordered structure than pristine graphene. A common organic solvent, DMSO, was used as the S-source. Owing to its higher degree of disorder, the doped anode material exhibited stable capacity retention up to 1000 cycles. Additionally, due to the presence of a large number of active sites for Na storage, the doped anode exhibited better electrochemical performance than the undoped one. A 3D free-standing NG aerogel has also been proposed as the anode material for SIBs [406].

The problem of poor conductivity of the metal sulfides as electrode materials has been solved by anchoring them on the surface of NG. For example, an anode material for SIBs has been demonstrated by anchoring Fe7S8 nanoparticles on the NG surface [407]. The authors have also studied how dopant loading can affect the battery performance of the composite anodes. While the high mass loading resulted in uneven distribution of metal sulfide particles, low mass loading led to lower capacity. The capacity loss after the first few cycles was explained by electrolyte decomposition and unstable SEI formation, which blocked the ion/electron transport. In spite of these problems, by enhancing the charge-transfer kinetics, the composite electrodes exhibited satisfactory rate capability and cycling stability.

In another study, a 3D Fe, N, S doped graphene network has been used as the oxygen electrocatalyst for Zn-air batteries [408]. Applied as the air electrode, the doped graphene exhibited higher output power density than Pt/C commercial electrodes. The hydrophilic nature of the doped electrode increased electron adsorption during the electrochemical process, enhancing battery performance. Gursu et al. [409] studied the electrochemical behavior of Cl-doped graphene electrodes for vanadium redox flow batteries. Synthesized by cyclic voltammetry, this doped graphene-based positive electrode achieved better battery performance in a vanadium–containing mixed electrolyte (a mixture of VOSO4 2.0 M and H2SO4 5.0 M). The S-doped graphene has also been used as positive electrode for vanadium redox flow batteries [410]. With high electrochemical active surface area, the S-doped electrode exhibited better battery performance than its undoped counterpart.

Fuel cells are the most promising devices for delivering the high electrical power densities needed for many key applications. They accomplish the task by converting the energy stored in chemical bonds into electrical energy. Carbon materials with high surface area are regarded as efficient supports for electrocatalysts in fuel cells. With improved conductivity and charge transfer properties, doped graphene derivatives are expected to further enhance the performance of fuel cells when applied as catalyst supports [411–415].

BG has been used as a stable Pt catalyst support and electrode in a solid acid fuel cell (SAFC) described by Tennyson et al. [321]. The BG flakes, synthesized by pulsed laser ablation, efficiently worked in a SAFC (with cesium dihydrogen phosphate electrolyte) operated at 250 °C. The reduction in the electron density of graphene upon B-doping decreased the rate of O2 chemisorption and stabilized the carbon electrodes in the harsh environment of the SAFC. Kabir et al. [416] studied the performance of NG in fuel cell electrodes. They changed the concentration of different N-moieties in graphene and compared the ORR activities. Doped graphene with high levels of hydrogenated-N and low concentration of pyridinic-N exhibited the best performance regarding current density, onset potential and half-wave potential. Based on their experimental results, the authors proposed the ORR mechanism for each N-moiety. Dong et al. [307] reported the enhanced ORR performance of (B, N, P, Fe)-doped porous graphene foam. Using H3BO3, (C6H5)2P, CN2H2, and FeCl2 as precursors, they prepared the multi-doped graphene by a hard-templating method. The authors tested the stability of the electrodes in different media, in order to verify their practical use. When tested at constant potential, the minimal decrease in half-wave potential demonstrated the improved stability of the catalyst in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes. Due to its high surface area, mesoporous structure and synergistic effects, the multiple element-doped graphene foam showed higher catalytic activity than its single or dual-doped counterparts.

The Pt nanoparticles anchored to doped graphene have been applied as catalysts for different types of fuel cells. For example, Pt nanoparticles supported on P–S co-doped graphene were reported to boost the kinetics of the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR), which is one of the significant challenges for direct methanol fuel cells [295]. From DFT calculations, the authors observed that the change in charge density upon doping enhanced the interaction between Pt and carbon, due to the formation of P–C bonds. The co-doped catalyst exhibited higher electrochemical surface area and better MOR activity and stability than single-doped graphene. The ORR catalytic activity of NG has been theoretically examined by Reda et al. [417]. The authors observed a greater stabilization of *O and *O2 adsorbates on the basal plane. Through DFT calculations, they concluded that the presence of additional free N and solvation effects were responsible for the improved performance when compared to metal-based catalysts.

The ORR catalytic activity of 3D NG-supported Pd composites for anion exchange membrane fuel cells has been studied by Kabir et al. [418]. The 3D doped electrode exhibited higher onset potential for ORR and higher power density than Pd/carbon black and other graphene-based nanocomposites. The enhanced performance was attributed to the improved mass transfer kinetics and the electronic properties of the support. Co-doped N–S graphene has been prepared by a soft template-assisted method using C3H6N6 and benzyl disulfide [243]. The material exhibited good methanol tolerance and long-term durability as a catalyst for fuel cells. The enhanced performance was attributed to the high surface area, porous nano-architecture. Transition metal oxy-hydroxides also demonstrated better ORR activity when associated with NG. For example, a porous NG-encapsulated β-FeOOH hybrid has been reported by Li et al. [419]. Active sites were derived from the surface FeO6 positions, reducing the free energy gap for the ORR reaction and enhancing the catalytic performance. Used as cathode in both microbial fuel cells and direct methanol fuel cells, the hybrid displayed better performance than a commercial Pt/C catalyst in acidic, neutral, and basic media.

In doped graphene, the transition metals and oxides are usually associated with the heteroatoms in the carbon network, showing good potential as fuel cell electrode materials. For example, NG nanoplatelets with Fe have been reported by Kim et al. [420] for proton exchange membrane fuel cells working in acidic media. In a recent study, Varga et al. [421] investigated the electrocatalytic activity of Co4N/NG hybrids for alkaline fuel cells. The composites exhibited better methanol tolerance than a commercial Pt/C catalyst. A ternary hybrid based on Fe2O3, MoS2 and NG has been proposed by Chuong et al. [422], in order to overcome the problem of methanol crossover in cathodic electrocatalysts. With high electron-transfer number (3.91–3.96), the catalyst demonstrated great working stability. Xu et al. [423] synthesized NG supported PtAu/Pt core/shell nanocrystals for electrocatalytic oxidation of formic acid. The authors claimed that, with enhanced mass and specific activity, the material can be considered as one of the most promising electrocatalysts. The use of NG as an efficient electrocatalyst for Zn-air batteries has been reported by Niu et al. [424]. They synthesized NG using precursor fibers made from a metal–organic framework, the zeolitic imidazolate framework-67 (ZIF-67). The doped graphene exhibited prominent ORR activity and high power density due to its high surface area and abundant electrolyte-accessible internal volumes.

Carbon-based materials have been extensively used in different types of solar cells, mainly due to the high electron mobilities of graphene and rGO. By effectively improving the physicochemical properties of current collectors (hole and electron extraction layers), graphene doping has further enhanced the performance of solar cells [124,425–429]. A review article recently published by Hu et al. [430] provides an expert overview of the applications of doped graphene in different types of solar cells.

B-rGO paper, synthesized by annealing with B2O3 as B source, has been used as the hole transport material in perovskite solar cells [122]. A flexible perovskite solar cells with sandwich structure was fabricated (FTO/TiO2/CH3NH3PbI3/B-rGO paper/FTO), delivering high power conversion efficiency (PCE). With the improved performance, the doped graphene paper has the potential to replace both the organic-based hole transport layer (HTL) and the expensive Au counter electrode (CE). In another report, Zhao et al. [431] concluded that doped graphene may replace the high-cost Pt CE in DSSCs. Using density functional theory, the authors found that p-type doped graphene shows lower overpotential and better catalytic performance in the tri-iodide reduction reaction (IRR) than Pt CEs.

Doped graphene-based hybrid nanocomposites have also been used as components in solar cells. As an example, SG/FeS2 microspheres were used as the CE in DSSCs [432]. In this work the authors concluded that, among a series of heteroatoms (I, P, B, N, S), graphene doped with S exhibited good characteristics for IRR. The hybrid electrocatalyst based on SG and FeS2 showed higher catalytic activity and electrochemical stability than the standard Pt CE. Nanocomposites based on graphene and metal oxides have also been investigated as CEs for DSSCs. A flower-like mesoporous Fe3O4 wrapped with NG exhibited good catalytic performance for IRR [187]. The full cell based on the hybrid showed high photovoltaic activity and electrochemical stability. Ternary nanohybrids based on doped graphene have also been reported for use in DSSCs. For example, Yao et al. [187] demonstrated the improved catalytic activity of core–shell structured ternary carbon mesoporous spheres/Fe3O4/NG composite as CE in DSSCs. The ternary hybrid showed higher PCE than a Pt CE due to synergistic effects among the components. Metal alloys have also been combined with doped graphene to fabricate the CEs for DSSCs. A colloidal nanocomposite based on TiN and NG, synthesized from the molecular precursors, exhibited localized surface plasmon resonance, high electrical conductivity, and large surface area [433]. These properties from the colloidal composite enabled the fabrication of DSSCs with high PCE values (8.9%). The authors have mentioned that the role of NG was not only to provide high surface area and better charge transport, but also to improve the colloidal stability.

Heteroatom-doped graphene materials have also been combined with other 2D materials for application in solar cell electrodes. For example, Wang et al. [434] synthesized an S-rGO/MoS2 composite by thermal treatments, using CS2 as the S-source. The composite electrode exhibited PCE values comparable to those of Pt CEs. The combination of two 2D materials created more exposed edges and active sites for the IRR process. Selenium-doped graphene has also been applied to DSSCs [435]. Working as the CE, Se-doped graphene exhibited better PCE values than Pt CEs. The improved electrochemical performance of the material was attributed to the reduced charge transfer resistance, resulting from the low ionization energy of the defects induced in the graphene.

Doped graphene-based materials have also been introduced in FOSCs. Shin et al. [436] discussed the construction of a FOSC incorporating bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)-amide doped graphene as anode and triethylenetetramine doped graphene as cathode. The FOSC exhibited moderate PCE, but retained 99% efficiency after being subjected to bending tests. Quantum dots have also been combined with doped graphene to fabricate high-performance solar cells. In a recent study, Amollo et al. [437] demonstrated the applicability of Ge quantum dot/NG nanocomposites as active layers of bulk heterojunction solar cells. With better charge transport properties through the formation of interconnected pathways between doped graphene and metal quantum dots, the device exhibited high PCE.

Hu et al. [438] have demonstrated an integrated energy system combining a CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite solar cell, a photoelectrochemical water splitting device, and an H2-O2 fuel cell. A co-doped N–S 3D graphitic network worked in the electrochemical devices as a tri-functional catalyst for ORR/OER/HER. This work proposed a unique approach for the economical generation of clean and renewable electricity, starting from sunlight and water. However, commercialization of this type of integrated energy system still requires a great deal of optimization work, focused mainly on performance enhancement and quality control.

In order to have a reliable supply of hydrogen and oxygen to for the rapidly developing fuel cell technology, researchers have turned their attention to water splitting. Electrochemical water splitting is deemed a sustainable method for obtaining hydrogen (by HER) and oxygen (by OER). The catalysts commonly used for both reactions are noble metals/metal oxides such as Pt, Pd or IrO2 [110]. The very high cost and scarcity of the noble metals has prompted the search for non-precious electrocatalysts with superior activity and stability for use in the water splitting process. Several researchers have tested heteroatom-doped graphene-based materials for this purpose [111,439–446]. Non-metal heteroatom-doped graphene has been reported to improve the catalytic performance for both HER and OER. The synergistic effects generated in composites combining heteroatom-doped graphene-based materials and transition metal nanoparticles further improve electrocatalytic performance [447].

N–S co-doped graphene as a candidate for photocatalytic electrochemical water splitting studied by Li et al. [110]. The material was defect-rich, with a large specific surface area. N-doping has been reported to impart exceptional electrocatalytic water splitting activity, while co-doping with S further enhanced the charge/spin distribution in the carbon lattice. In their comparison of as-obtained N–S co-doped graphene with chemically activated N–S co-doped graphene, they reported an overpotential reduction from 0.407 V to 0.292 V at −10 mA/cm2 in 1 M KOH solution. Zhu et al. [448] have investigated the water splitting properties of composites integrating Co@Co3O4/Fe and N–S co-doped rGO. The material demonstrated outstanding electrocatalytic activity for HER, OER and ORR when compared to previously reported catalysts. The superior performance was attributed to heteroatom co-doping and the formation of a sandwich-like composite architecture. The adsorption of OH− and H+ in alkaline solutions was enhanced due to the presence of N and S atoms, which increased the number of active sites and improved electron transport. The addition of Co3O4 shells and Co@Co3O4 to the rGO sheets lowered the thermodynamic barrier for intermediate reactions, and leaching stability was further improved.

Heteroatom doping of GQDs for water splitting application has also been reported. Sim et al. [449] studied the formation of nitrogen–fluorine (N–F) co-doped GQDs by plasma treatment. The authors achieved overpotentials of 0.13 and 0.4 V for HER and OER, respectively, reflecting the lower activation energy required for hydrogen evolution. The synergetic effects from N and F coupling in the N–F co-doped GQDs resulted in low overpotential, high current density and long-term stability.

As exhaustively discussed in this review article, all the electrochemical energy conversion and storage devices can have their performance improved by doping. Fig. 7 shows a schematic drawing of a hypothetical integrated electrochemical system assembled with clean, renewable energy devices. All the electrodes used in these devices could be made with doped graphene and hybrid materials.

This review summarized recent developments andthe synthetic approaches for various kinds of doped graphene-based materials. Graphene doping by adsorption of atoms on the graphene surface eliminates the risk of disrupting the carbon network, so in this respect, it can be considered a preferred doping strategy. On the other hand, adsorption does not guarantee a very stable material. In this regard, different lines of attack, like adjusting the doping levels, tuning the hole/electron concentration, etc. should be systematically investigated.

A variety of doping strategies have already been proposed for tailoring the structure/properties of graphene materials for energy applications. However, there are still several challenges related with heteroatom doping in graphene materials which have not yet been solved. Examples of important remaining issues are:
i.
Even though N-doping of graphene may improve its properties, making the material very attractive for various applications, the large scale production of NG still remains a great challenge.
ii.
Graphene co-doping strategies unlocked a new area of research. The approach is very interesting for energy-related applications, but an in-depth understanding of the detailed mechanisms actually responsible for the observed improvement in the properties of co-doped materials is still needed.
iii.
It is important to notice that, for practical energy-related applications, it is still necessary to determine, first of all, which specific elements would be the best choice for doping graphene. For example, it has been established that N enhances the capacitive properties of graphene materials, making them attractive for SC applications.
iv.
It is obvious that defects are induced in graphene through the doping process, and this is a welcome outcome in most cases. On the other hand, the creation of an excessive number of defects and structural distortion during synthesis can decrease the electronic mobility of doped graphene materials, hampering their physical properties. In this regard, new synthetic approaches which are able to yield higher quality doped graphene are highly desirable.
v.
A noteworthy improvement in the performance of a significant number of devices has been achieved through the application of heteroatom-doped graphene materials. However, in-depth knowledge of the bonding details between dopant atoms and graphene has not yet been fully acquired.
vi.
Despite the variety of doping methods already reported for synthesizing doped graphene materials, the production of large sized doped graphene sheets is still a very demanding task.
vii.
Controlling the precise concentration of heteroatoms in doped graphene materials, while at the same time guaranteeing their uniform distribution across the carbon network remains a big challenge for achieving better performance in energy-related devices.
viii.
Knowledge of the doping mechanisms (especially for dual-doped graphene), and the comprehensive effects of different co-dopant ratios on the electrochemical properties are not yet up to the standards required. For example, the reasons for the increment in ORR activity through heteroatom-doping are not yet completely clear, demanding further investigation.
ix.
New and more convenient synthesis protocols at lower processing temperatures should be developed for controlling the concentration of doping elements and the number of layers in graphene materials.

In summary, although great strides have already been taken in the study, development and application of heteroatom-doped graphene materials for energy-related applications, more theoretical and experimental work is still needed for the skilled engineering of doped graphene materials for efficient future electrochemical devices.
